1.hint: Round, no corners — name the shape.
1.help:
A **circle** is round with **no vertices**.

Example: wheel outline.

2.hint: Middle point of a circle — special name?
2.help:
The **centre** is the middle point equidistant from the edge.

Example: compass needle point.

3.hint: Every point on the edge — same distance from centre?
3.help:
Yes — all points on the **circumference** are the same distance from the centre. **True**.

Example: radius constant round the circle.

4.hint: Centre to edge — radius or diameter?
4.help:
Centre to edge is a **radius**.

Example: spoke on a wheel.

Picture what is happening, then use the method step by step in your working.

5.hint: Across through the centre — radius or diameter?
5.help:
Through the centre edge to edge is a **diameter**.

Example: widest part of a circle.

6.help:
Diameter $= 2 \times 3 = 6$ cm.

Example: radius 5 cm → diameter 10 cm.

7.help:
Radius $= 10 \div 2 = 5$ cm.

Example: diameter 14 cm → radius 7 cm.

8.hint: Outside edge of a circle — one word.
8.help:
The **circumference** is the distance around the outside.

Example: rim of a cup.

9.help:
The **centre**.

Example: dot at middle of a circle.

Picture what is happening, then use the method step by step in your working.

10.help:
**Radius** — distance from centre to circumference.

Example: 4 cm radius.

11.help:
**Diameter** — twice the radius.

Example: 12 cm diameter → 6 cm radius.

12.help:
Diameter $= 2 \times$ **radius**.

Example: $d = 2r$.

Picture what is happening, then use the method step by step in your working.

13.hint: Distance all the way round a circle.
13.help:
**Circumference** — perimeter of a circle.

Example: measure rim length.

14.hint: Touches at one point only, does not cut through.
14.help:
A **tangent** touches the circle at **one point**.

Example: ladder just touching a round tower.

15.hint: Straight line joining two points on the edge.
15.help:
A **chord** joins two points on the **circumference**.

Example: string stretched between two rim points.

16.hint: Part of the curved edge between two points.
16.help:
An **arc** is part of the **circumference** between two points.

Example: rainbow arc.

17.hint: Two radii and an arc — pizza slice name.
17.help:
A **sector** — region between two radii and an arc.

Example: pizza slice from centre.

18.hint: Region between a chord and an arc — not through centre.
18.help:
A **segment** — area between **chord** and **arc**.

Example: cap cut off a circle.

19.hint: Diameter joins two points on the circle — is it a chord?
19.help:
A diameter is a chord through the **centre** — **true**.

Example: longest chord.

20.hint: Tangent and radius at contact point — what angle?
20.help:
**90°** — tangent **perpendicular** to radius at touch point.

Example: radius to tangent forms right angle.

21.hint: Segment from centre to edge — name?
21.help:
**Radius**.

Example: 7 cm radius.

Picture what is happening, then use the method step by step in your working.

22.hint: Line segment with both ends on the circle.
22.help:
A **chord** connects two points on the circumference.

Example: not necessarily through centre.

23.help:
Diameter $= 2 \times 6 = 12$ cm.

Example: r=8 → d=16.

Picture what is happening, then use the method step by step in your working.

24.hint: Why 90° at tangent touch point — shortest distance to line.
24.help:
The radius to the touch point is the **shortest** line to the tangent, so it meets at **90°**.

Example: sketch radius to tangent.

25.hint: Chord splits circle into two regions with arc + chord.
25.help:
Two **segments** (minor and major) — each bounded by chord and arc.

Example: chord not through centre → unequal segments.

26.hint: Longest chord passes through centre — always?
26.help:
**Diameter** is longest chord — **always** true.

Example: any other chord shorter.

27.hint: Two tangents from outside point to circle — compare lengths.
27.help:
Tangent segments from one **external point** are **equal** in length.

Example: two tangents from P to circle.

28.hint: Radius to centre; chord edge to edge — not the same.
28.help:
Sam is **wrong**: **radius** centre to edge; **chord** between two edge points.

Example: radius 5 cm vs chord 8 cm.

29.hint: 90° sector — what fraction of full 360° turn?
29.help:
$90 \div 360 = \frac{1}{4}$ of the circle.

Example: quarter pizza.

30.hint: Sector includes centre; segment does not.
30.help:
**Sector**: two **radii** + arc (pie slice). **Segment**: **chord** + arc (no centre corner).

Example: label both on a diagram.

31.help:
Radius $= 1.4 \div 2 = 0.7$ m.

Example: table diameter 2 m → r=1 m.

32.hint: Path once around the track — distance name.
32.help:
One lap is the **circumference**.

Example: tyre tracks full loop.

33.hint: Two tangents from outside — equal tangent lengths.
33.help:
Tangent segments from the external point to the circle are **equal**.

Example: symmetric struts to rim.

34.help:
Centre angle each slice $= 360 \div 6 = 60°$.

Example: 8 slices → 45° each.

35.help:
Diameter $= 2 \times 8 = 16$ cm.

Example: through centre and minute hand tip.

36.hint: Straight line across semicircle flat end.
36.help:
The flat end is a **diameter** (or **chord** of full circle).

Example: semicircle base line.

37.hint: Shortest from centre to tangent — along radius at 90°.
37.help:
Shortest distance is the **radius** to the touch point, perpendicular to tangent.

Example: compare slant line vs radius.

38.hint: External touch — centres apart by sum of radii.
38.help:
Distance between centres $= 5 + 3 = 8$ cm.

Example: two circles touching externally.

39.hint: Chord 6 cm from centre, radius 10 — half-chord 3, Pythagoras.
39.help:
Half chord $= 3$ cm. Chord length $= 2 \times \sqrt{10^2 - 6^2} = 2 \times 8 = 16$ cm.

Example: right triangle radius, distance, half-chord.

40.hint: Recall the tangent–radius theorem at the touching point.
40.help:
A tangent touches a circle at exactly one point. The radius drawn to that point is perpendicular to the tangent.

Perpendicular lines meet at a right angle, which is 90°.

41.help:
Diameter **10 cm**.

Example: r=5 → d=10.

Picture what is happening, then use the method step by step in your working.

42.help:
**Tangent**.

Example: line just grazing circle.

Picture what is happening, then use the method step by step in your working.

43.hint: Which circle word matches: centre to the circle?
43.help: A **radius** is the part that centre to the circle. Sketch a circle and label it.

44.hint: Which circle word matches: through the centre, end to end?
44.help: A **diameter** is the part that through the centre, end to end. Sketch a circle and label it.

45.hint: Diameter is twice the radius — double 9 cm.
45.help:
Diameter $= 2 \times 9 = 18$ cm.

Example: radius 6 cm → diameter 12 cm.

46.hint: Radius is half the diameter — divide 14 cm by 2.
46.help:
Radius $= 14 \div 2 = 7$ cm.

Example: diameter 20 cm → radius 10 cm.

47.hint: A diameter joins two points on the circle through the centre — is it also a chord?
47.help:
A **diameter** is a **chord** that passes through the **centre** — **true**.

Example: any chord is a line segment between two points on the circle.

48.hint: Which line touches the circle at exactly one point without crossing inside?
48.help:
A **tangent** touches the circle at **one point** only.

Example: a wheel on flat ground — ground line is tangent at the contact.

49.hint: All radii of one circle go from centre to edge — are they the same length?
49.help:
Every **radius** of the same circle has the **same length** — **true**.

Example: radius 5 cm from centre to any point on the circle.

50.hint: Sector fraction: divide central angle 72° by 360°.
50.help:
Fraction $= 72 \div 360 = 1/5$ of the full circle.

Example: 90° sector → $90/360 = 1/4$.

51.hint: Eight equal pizza slices from the centre — divide 360° by 8.
51.help:
Central angle per slice $= 360 \div 8 = 45°$.

Example: 6 equal slices → 60° each.

52.hint: Radius meets tangent at the contact point — what angle do they make?
52.help:
Radius and tangent are **perpendicular** → **90°** at the contact point.

Example: a square corner fits between radius and tangent.

53.hint: The diameter is the longest chord — can any chord be longer than a diameter?
53.help:
No chord can be **longer than a diameter** — **never**.

Example: diameter 10 cm is the maximum chord length.

54.hint: Circumference uses $\pi d$ — find diameter from radius 11 cm first.
54.help:
Diameter $= 2 \times 11 = 22$ cm, then use $C = \pi d$.

Example: radius 5 cm → $d = 10$ cm.

55.hint: From an external point, two tangents are drawn to a circle — how do their lengths compare?
55.help:
The two tangents from the same external point are **equal in length**.

Example: both tangents from P might be 8 cm each.

56.hint: Semicircle flat edge across the middle — what is that straight line called?
56.help:
The flat edge of a semicircle is the **diameter**.

Example: half a circle cut through the centre.

57.hint: Does the centre point lie on every chord of a circle?
57.help:
Only a **diameter** passes through the centre — ordinary chords need **not** contain the centre — **false**.

Example: a short chord misses the centre.

58.hint: Compare chord 8 cm with diameter 10 cm (radius 5 cm) — which is longer?
58.help:
Diameter $= 10$ cm. Chord **8 cm < 10 cm**, so the chord is **shorter**.

Example: longest chord equals diameter.

59.hint: Sector with central angle 120° — what fraction of 360°?
59.help:
Fraction $= 120 \div 360 = 1/3$ of the circle.

Example: 180° sector is half the circle.

60.hint: Two circles with the same radius — identical size circles?
60.help:
Equal radii → same size circles → **congruent** — **always**.

Example: both radius 4 cm circles match exactly.

61.hint: Quarter turn of minute hand — what fraction of the full circumference does the tip travel?
61.help:
Quarter turn $= 1/4$ of the circumference. Arc length $= \frac{1}{4} \times 2\pi r$ with $r = 10$ cm.

Example: half turn → half the circumference.

62.hint: Two tangents from the same point P — if one is 12 cm, what is the other?
62.help:
Equal tangents property: second tangent is also **12 cm**.

Example: both tangents from P are 7 cm.

63.hint: Is an arc a curved part of the circumference?
63.help:
An **arc** is part of the **circumference** — **true**.

Example: the curved edge from A to B on a circle.

64.hint: Radius is half the diameter — find radius when diameter is 20 cm.
64.help:
Radius $= 20 \div 2 = 10$ cm.

Example: diameter 14 cm → radius 7 cm.

65.hint: Segment vs sector — which region includes the centre of the circle?
65.help:
A **sector** has two radii and includes the **centre**. A **segment** is between a chord and arc — usually **no centre**.

Example: pizza slice is a sector.

66.hint: If a radius is perpendicular to a chord, does it cut the chord in half?
66.help:
A radius **perpendicular** to a chord **bisects** the chord — **always** in the same circle.

Example: symmetry through the centre.

67.hint: Circles touch externally — add radii 4 cm and 7 cm for centre distance.
67.help:
Distance between centres $= 4 + 7 = 11$ cm.

Example: radii 3 and 5 touching externally → 8 cm apart.

68.hint: 90° sector — what fraction of the full circle area?
68.help:
Fraction $= 90 \div 360 = 1/4$ of the circle area.

Example: 180° sector is half the area.

69.hint: Are all points on a circle the same distance from the centre?
69.help:
Every point on a circle is **equidistant** from the **centre** — **true**.

Example: that distance is the radius.

70.hint: OA joins centre O to point A on the circle — what is OA called?
70.help:
**OA** is a **radius** (centre to circle).

Example: any line from centre to the edge is a radius.

71.hint: Radius 13 cm — double it for the diameter.
71.help:
Diameter $= 2 \times 13 = 26$ cm.

Example: radius 8 cm → diameter 16 cm.

72.hint: Can a tangent line cross the circle at two points?
72.help:
A **tangent** touches at **one point** only — it cannot cut twice — **never**.

Example: tangent just grazes the circle.

73.hint: Longest chord in a circle — what special name does it have?
73.help:
The **longest chord** is the **diameter** $= 2 \times 8 = 16$ cm.

Example: radius 5 cm → longest chord 10 cm.

74.hint: 60° sector — divide by 360° for fraction of circumference.
74.help:
Arc fraction $= 60 \div 360 = 1/6$ of the circumference.

Example: 90° → $1/4$ of circumference.

75.hint: Does circumference mean the distance around the edge of a circle?
75.help:
**Circumference** is the perimeter around a circle — **true**.

Example: measure the edge of a plate.

76.hint: Equal tangents from P — if one tangent is 9 cm, find the other.
76.help:
Both tangents from P are **9 cm**.

Example: tangents 6 cm and 6 cm from same point.

77.hint: Diameter from radius 12 cm — multiply by 2.
77.help:
Diameter $= 2 \times 12 = 24$ cm.

Example: radius 9 cm → diameter 18 cm.

78.hint: Must every diameter pass through the centre of the circle?
78.help:
A **diameter** goes through the **centre** end to end — **always**.

Example: centre is the midpoint of a diameter.

79.hint: Chord 10 cm in a circle whose diameter is 10 cm — is it a diameter?
79.help:
A chord equal to the **diameter** **is** a diameter — **yes**.

Example: longest possible chord equals diameter.

80.hint: Sector angle 45° — fraction of full 360° turn?

81.hint: Does a sector look like a slice of pizza from the centre?
81.help:
A **sector** is a **pizza-slice** shape with two radii and an arc — **true**.

Example: wedge cut from centre of round pizza.

82.hint: Circles touch internally — subtract smaller radius 3 cm from larger 5 cm.
82.help:
Centre distance $= 5 - 3 = 2$ cm.

Example: radii 8 and 5 internally → 3 cm apart.

83.hint: Find diameter when radius is 7 cm.
83.help:
Diameter $= 2 \times 7 = 14$ cm.

Example: radius 4.5 cm → diameter 9 cm.

84.hint: Tangents drawn from the same point outside the circle — equal lengths?
84.help:
Tangents from one **external point** are **equal** — **always**.

Example: both 11 cm from P.

85.hint: Minor arc AB has a longer partner from A to B — what is it called?
85.help:
The longer path is the **major arc**.

Example: minor arc is the shorter curve between A and B.

86.hint: Radius to the tangent contact point — perpendicular to the tangent?
86.help:
Radius ⟂ tangent at the point of contact — **true** (90°).

Example: square corner at contact point.

87.hint: Diameter 30 cm — is chord 24 cm shorter than the diameter?
87.help:
24 cm **<** 30 cm diameter — chord is **shorter** — **yes**.

Example: no chord exceeds diameter length.

88.hint: Central angle 180° sector — half a circle — what shape name?
88.help:
180° sector is a **semicircle**.

Example: half a round pizza.

89.hint: Can two different chords in the same circle have the same length?
89.help:
**Equal chords** are possible in one circle — **always** (symmetry).

Example: two 6 cm chords opposite each other.

90.hint: Line from centre O to point A on the circle — name OA.
90.help:
A segment from the **centre** to any point on the circle is a **radius**. So **OA** is a radius.

Example: OB or OC from the centre are also radii.

91.hint: Is a segment the region trapped between a chord and an arc?
91.help:
A **segment** is the region between a **chord** and an **arc** — **true**.

Example: a flat cap cut off a circle.

92.hint: Diameter 1.8 m — halve it and convert metres to centimetres.
92.help:
Radius $= 0.9$ m $= 90$ cm.

Example: diameter 2 m → radius 100 cm.

93.hint: External touch — add radii 6 cm and 2 cm for distance between centres.
93.help:
Distance $= 6 + 2 = 8$ cm.

Example: radii 5 and 4 external → 9 cm.

94.hint: Line through centre meeting circle at both ends — is it a diameter?
94.help:
A line through the **centre** hitting the circle twice is a **diameter** — **always**.

Example: widest chord through centre.

95.hint: Angle between radius and tangent at the point of contact?
95.help:
Tangent ⟂ radius → **90°**.

Example: square corner at contact point.

96.hint: Is $\pi$ used in the formula for circle circumference?
96.help:
$C = \pi d$ or $C = 2\pi r$ — **true** that **π** is used.

Example: circumference of a wheel uses π.

97.hint: 30° sector — what fraction of a full 360° turn?

98.hint: Longest possible chord when radius is 20 cm?
98.help:
Longest chord $=$ **diameter** $= 40$ cm.

Example: radius 7 cm → longest chord 14 cm.

99.hint: Concentric circles — do they share the same centre?
99.help:
**Concentric** circles have the **same centre** — **always**.

Example: target rings on a dartboard.

100.hint: Chord 6 cm, radius 5 cm — half chord 3, radius 5: can a right triangle work?
100.help:
Half chord 3, radius 5: $3^2 + 4^2 = 5^2$ — distance from centre to chord is 4 cm — **yes, possible**.

Example: 3-4-5 triangle inside circle.

101.hint: Where does the centre of a circle sit — inside or outside?
101.help:
The **centre** is **inside** the circle — **true**.

Example: compass needle point sits inside the drawn circle.

102.hint: Diameter 22 cm — halve it to find the radius.
102.help:
Radius $= 22 \div 2 = 11$ cm.

Example: diameter 16 cm → radius 8 cm.

103.hint: Two tangents both 15 cm from P — are tangents from one point equal?
103.help:
Tangents from the same **external point** are **equal** — **true**.

Example: both 9 cm from P.

104.hint: Half a circle — what fraction of the full circumference?
104.help:
Half a circle is **$1/2$** of the circumference.

Example: semicircle arc is half the edge.

105.hint: Does a sector always have two radii from the centre?
105.help:
A **sector** is bounded by **two radii** and an arc — **always**.

Example: pizza slice has two straight edges from centre.

106.hint: Two radii 4 cm apart at 90° from the centre — name the bounded shape.
106.help:
Two radii at **90°** make a **quarter circle** (quadrant).

Example: one slice of a four-slice pizza.

107.hint: Does a chord join two points on the circle's edge?
107.help:
A **chord** joins **two points on the circle** — **true**.

Example: straight line across inside a circle.

108.hint: External touch — add radii 10 cm and 3 cm for centre distance.
108.help:
Distance $= 10 + 3 = 13$ cm.

Example: radii 7 and 4 external → 11 cm.

109.hint: 144° sector — divide by 360° for the fraction of the circle.

110.hint: Is diameter always twice the radius?
110.help:
Diameter $= 2 \times$ radius — **always**.

Example: radius 5 cm → diameter 10 cm.

111.hint: Radius OT to tangent at T — what angle between them?
111.help:
Radius ⟂ tangent at contact → **90°**.

Example: square corner at T.

112.hint: Which arc is shorter — minor or major?
112.help:
The **minor arc** is the **shorter** path along the circumference — **true**.

Example: minor arc is the small curve between A and B.

113.hint: Diameter 50 cm — halve to get radius.
113.help:
Radius $= 50 \div 2 = 25$ cm.

Example: diameter 18 cm → radius 9 cm.

114.hint: Equal tangents from P — if one is 7.2 cm, find the other.
114.help:
Both tangents from P are **7.2 cm**.

Example: tangents 5 cm and 5 cm from same point.

115.hint: Can radii of the same circle have different lengths?
115.help:
All **radii** of one circle are **equal** — **always**.

Example: every spoke same length on a wheel.

116.hint: How many 40° sectors fit into a full 360° turn?
116.help:
$360 \div 40 = 9$ sectors fit.

Example: eight 45° slices make a full pizza.

117.hint: Is a semicircle both a segment and a sector?
117.help:
A **semicircle** is both a **segment** and a **sector** — **true**.

Example: half pizza is slice and cap shape.

118.hint: Radius 6 cm — double it for diameter.
118.help:
Diameter $= 2 \times 6 = 12$ cm.

Example: radius 10 cm → diameter 20 cm.

119.hint: Same centre, radii 5 and 8 — are the circles concentric?
119.help:
Same **centre**, different radii → **concentric** — **yes**.

Example: dartboard rings share one centre.

120.hint: Does a tangent stay outside the circle except at one touch point?
120.help:
A **tangent** touches at **one point** only — rest is outside — **always**.

Example: line just grazing a ball.

121.hint: Chord 12 cm, diameter 20 cm — is the chord shorter than the diameter?
121.help:
12 cm **<** 20 cm diameter — chord is **shorter** — **yes**.

Example: no chord beats diameter length.

122.help:
$C = \pi d$ — **true**.

Example: wheel rim length uses $\pi \times$ width.

123.hint: 270° sector — what fraction of the full 360° circle?

124.hint: Both tangents from P are 4 cm — what property links them?
124.help:
**Equal tangents** from an external point — both **4 cm**.

Example: tangents from P always match in length.

125.hint: Is the longest chord in any circle always a diameter?
125.help:
The **longest chord** is the **diameter** — **always**.

Example: widest line through centre.

126.hint: Radius 25 cm — multiply by 2 for diameter.
126.help:
Diameter $= 2 \times 25 = 50$ cm.

Example: radius 12 cm → diameter 24 cm.

127.hint: Does an arc start and end at points on the circle?
127.help:
An **arc** has **two endpoints** on the circle — **true**.

Example: curved edge between A and B.

128.hint: External touch — add radii 9 cm and 4 cm.
128.help:
Distance $= 9 + 4 = 13$ cm.

Example: radii 6 and 5 external → 11 cm.

129.hint: One fifth of a circle — what central angle in degrees?
129.help:
$360 \div 5 = 72°$ at the centre.

Example: eight equal slices → 45° each.

130.hint: Is the radius always perpendicular to the tangent at the contact point?
130.help:
Radius ⟂ tangent at contact — **always** (90°).

Example: square corner where radius meets tangent.

131.hint: Why are both tangents from an outside point the same length?
131.help:
From external point **P**, tangents to a circle are **equal** — symmetric right triangles OTP and OTP′.

Example: both tangents 6 cm from P.

132.hint: Does a sector include the centre of the circle?
132.help:
A **sector** is a wedge from the **centre** — **true**.

Example: pizza slice point is at the centre.

133.hint: Diameter 3.2 m — halve and convert to centimetres.
133.help:
Radius $= 1.6$ m $= 160$ cm.

Example: diameter 2.4 m → radius 120 cm.

134.hint: Chord that does not pass through the centre — still called a chord?
134.help:
A chord **not through the centre** is still a **chord** (not a diameter).

Example: short line across the circle off-centre.

135.hint: Can two circles touch at exactly one point?
135.help:
Circles can **touch externally or internally** at **one point** — **always** possible.

Example: two coins just touching edge to edge.

136.hint: 18° sector — divide by 360° for the fraction.

137.hint: Is every point on the circle the same distance from the centre?
137.help:
The **centre** is **equidistant** from all points on the circle — **true**.

Example: radius constant round the edge.

138.hint: Radius 14 cm — longest chord equals diameter.
138.help:
Longest chord $= 2 \times 14 = 28$ cm.

Example: radius 9 cm → longest chord 18 cm.

139.hint: Diameter perpendicular to a chord — does it bisect the chord?
139.help:
A **diameter perpendicular to a chord** **bisects** it — **true**.

Example: fold line through centre cuts chord in half.

140.hint: Equal tangents from P with radius ⟂ tangent — what quadrilateral is OTP?
140.help:
Two right angles at T and T′ with equal tangents → **kite** (or symmetric quadrilateral OTP…).

Example: mirror symmetry about OP.

141.hint: Does $\pi d$ give the circumference of a circle?

142.hint: Two radii 5 cm with 120° between them — name the sector OAB.
142.help:
Central angle **120°** makes a **sector** (one-third of a circle).

Example: three equal pizza slices fill 360°.

143.hint: Can a chord also be a diameter?
143.help:
A chord **through the centre** **is** a diameter — **always** possible.

Example: widest chord across the circle.

144.hint: Two circles both radius 2 cm — are they congruent?
144.help:
Same **radius** → **congruent circles** — **yes**.

Example: two identical coins.

145.hint: Does a segment usually include the centre?
145.help:
A **segment** (chord + arc region) usually **does not** include the centre — **true**.

Example: small cap cut off the top.

146.help:
Radius $= 18 \div 2 = 9$ cm.

Example: diameter 24 cm → radius 12 cm.

147.hint: External touch — add radii 12 cm and 5 cm.
147.help:
Distance $= 12 + 5 = 17$ cm.

Example: radii 8 and 3 external → 11 cm.

148.hint: Are tangents from P to a circle always symmetric about OP?
148.help:
Tangents from **P** are **symmetric** about line **OP** — **always**.

Example: mirror image left and right tangents.

149.hint: Arc is one quarter of the circle — what central angle?
149.help:
Quarter circle → central angle **90°**.

Example: four equal slices → 90° each.

150.hint: Sketch and name chord, tangent, and radius on one circle.
150.help:
**Radius**: centre to edge. **Chord**: line between two edge points. **Tangent**: touches edge once.

Example: label each on a wheel diagram.

151.hint: Is radius half of the diameter?
151.help:
Radius $= $ diameter $\div 2$ — **true**.

Example: diameter 14 cm → radius 7 cm.

153.hint: In one circle, do equal chords sit the same distance from the centre?
153.help:
**Equal chords** are **equidistant** from the centre in the same circle — **always**.

Example: two 8 cm chords at same depth.

154.hint: One tangent from P is 8 cm — find the other tangent length.
154.help:
Both tangents from P are **8 cm**.

Example: tangents 12 cm and 12 cm from same point.

155.hint: Is circumference the length around the outside edge?
155.help:
**Circumference** is the distance **around the edge** — **true**.

Example: tape measure round a tin.

156.help:
Diameter $= 2 \times 30 = 60$ cm.

Example: radius 15 cm → diameter 30 cm.

157.hint: Line through centre hitting the circle twice — is it a diameter?
157.help:
Through **centre**, meets circle twice → **diameter** — **always**.

Example: widest line across the circle.

158.hint: Central angle 200° — which sector is minor, 200° or 160°?
158.help:
Minor sector has the **smaller** angle: $360 - 200 = 160°$ is **minor** (200° is major).

Example: pick the smaller wedge.

159.hint: Concentric circles — do they have different radii?
159.help:
**Concentric** circles share a centre but have **different radii** — **true**.

Example: dartboard rings of different widths.

160.hint: Two circles radius 7 touch externally — distance between centres?
160.help:
Distance $= 7 + 7 = 14$ cm.

Example: two equal coins touching → sum of radii.

161.hint: Tangent meets radius at a square corner — explain with 90°.
161.help:
At the contact point, **radius ⟂ tangent** — like a **square corner** (90°).

Example: corner of a box where radius meets tangent line.

162.hint: Can any chord be longer than the diameter?
162.help:
The **diameter** is the **longest chord** — **always**.

Example: no line across the circle beats diameter length.

163.hint: Chord 16 cm equals diameter 16 cm — is it a diameter?
163.help:
Chord length **equals diameter** → it **is** a diameter — **yes**.

Example: widest chord through centre.

164.hint: Is a 90° sector exactly one quarter of the circle?
164.help:
90° out of 360° is **one quarter** — **true**.

Example: four equal pizza slices.

165.hint: Radius 4.5 cm — multiply by 2 for diameter.
165.help:
Diameter $= 2 \times 4.5 = 9$ cm.

Example: radius 3.5 cm → diameter 7 cm.

166.hint: Point P outside the circle — are tangents from P equal?
166.help:
From **external point P**, tangents are **equal** — **always**.

Example: both tangents 10 cm from P.

167.hint: Central angle 300° — find the minor sector angle.
167.help:
Minor sector angle $= 360 - 300 = 60°$.

Example: pick the smaller wedge under 180°.

168.hint: Does arc length depend on both radius and central angle?
168.help:
Arc length grows with **radius** and **angle at centre** — **true**.

Example: bigger circle or bigger angle → longer arc.

169.hint: Internal touch — subtract radii 10 cm and 6 cm.
169.help:
Distance $= 10 - 6 = 4$ cm.

Example: radii 9 and 4 internally → 5 cm apart.

170.hint: Draw a circle and label chord, tangent, and radius clearly.
170.help:
Sketch: **radius** centre-to-edge, **chord** between two edge points, **tangent** touching once.

Example: wheel with spoke, straight across, and grazing line.

171.hint: Do both endpoints of a diameter lie on the circle?
171.help:
Diameter endpoints are **on the circle** — **true**.

Example: widest line touches edge at both ends.

172.hint: Sector is one sixth of the circle — central angle?
172.help:
$360 \div 6 = 60°$ at the centre.

Example: six equal slices → 60° each.

173.hint: Can two circles cross at two separate points?
173.help:
Circles can **intersect at two points** — **always** possible if close enough.

Example: two overlapping rings.

174.hint: Equal tangents property — both lengths are 11 cm.
174.help:
Both tangents from P are **11 cm**.

Example: tangents 7 cm and 7 cm from same point.

175.hint: Does the centre give the circle its name and position?
175.help:
The **centre** fixes the circle's position — **true** (names the circle in diagrams).

Example: point O labels circle O.

176.help:
Radius $= 40 \div 2 = 20$ cm.

Example: diameter 26 cm → radius 13 cm.

177.hint: Radius to midpoint of a bisected chord — perpendicular?
177.help:
Radius to **midpoint of bisected chord** is **perpendicular** to the chord — **always**.

Example: fold line from centre cuts chord at 90°.

178.hint: Segment vs sector — which includes the centre?
178.help:
**Sector**: wedge from **centre** (two radii + arc). **Segment**: region between **chord and arc**, usually no centre.

Example: pizza slice vs flat cap.

179.help:
$\pi \approx 3.14$ — **true** (close enough for estimates).

Example: circumference ≈ $3.14 \times$ diameter.

180.hint: External touch — add radii 8 cm and 3 cm.
180.help:
Distance $= 8 + 3 = 11$ cm.

Example: radii 10 and 6 external → 16 cm.

181.hint: Can a tangent share two points with the circle?
181.help:
A **tangent** touches at **one point** only — **never** two.

Example: line grazing a ball at a single spot.

182.hint: How many 36° sectors fill a full 360° turn?
182.help:
$360 \div 36 = 10$ sectors.

Example: ten equal thin slices make a full circle.

183.hint: Does a diameter split a circle into two semicircles?
183.help:
A **diameter** cuts the circle into **two semicircles** — **true**.

Example: half a round pizza on each side.

184.hint: Half chord 5 cm, radius 6 cm — use Pythagoras for distance from centre.
184.help:
Right triangle: half chord 5, radius 6 → distance from centre $= \sqrt{6^2 - 5^2} = \sqrt{11}$ cm.

Example: 3-4-5 triangle gives distance 4 cm.

185.hint: Circles with equal radii — are they congruent?
185.help:
Equal **radii** → **congruent circles** — **always**.

Example: two coins of same size match exactly.

186.hint: Car mirror tangents both 2.1 m — find the other tangent length.
186.help:
Equal tangents → other length **2.1 m**.

Example: both tangents 1.5 m from same point.

187.hint: Does a sector's boundary include a curved arc?
187.help:
A **sector** boundary has an **arc** plus two radii — **true**.

Example: pizza slice curved outer edge is an arc.

188.hint: Radius 11 cm means diameter 22 cm — use that for circumference?
188.help:
Diameter $= 22$ cm, so $C = \pi \times 22$ — **true**.

Example: $C = \pi d$ with $d = 2r$.

189.hint: Does the word arc mean the curved part of the circumference?
189.help:
An **arc** is the **curved part** of the circumference — **always**.

Example: curved edge between A and B.

190.hint: Concentric circles radii 3 and 5 — subtract for ring width.
190.help:
Ring width $= 5 - 3 = 2$ cm.

Example: radii 4 and 7 → width 3 cm.

191.hint: Angle in a semicircle on the circumference — what size angle?
191.help:
Angle in a **semicircle** (on the circle, diameter as base) is **90°** — **true** (Thales).

Example: triangle on diameter with vertex on circle.

192.hint: 240° sector — what is the minor sector angle?
192.help:
Minor sector $= 360 - 240 = 120°$.

Example: pick the smaller wedge under 180°.

193.hint: Tangents from external point P — always equal in length?
193.help:
Tangents from **external P** are **equal** — **always**.

Example: both 8 cm from P.

194.hint: Five equal pizza slices — divide 360° by 5 for central angle.
194.help:
$360 \div 5 = 72°$ per slice.

Example: four slices → 90° each.

195.hint: Does a radius meet the circle at exactly one point?
195.help:
A **radius** meets the circle at **one point** on the edge — **true**.

Example: spoke touches rim once.

196.hint: Explain why diameter equals two radii placed end to end.
196.help:
Diameter goes **centre to edge twice** (both directions) → **diameter $= 2 \times$ radius**.

Example: two 5 cm radii make 10 cm diameter.

197.hint: Centres 13 cm apart, radii 5 and 8 — do they touch externally?
197.help:
$5 + 8 = 13$ cm — centres exactly **sum of radii** → **touch externally** — **yes**.

Example: two coins just touching.

198.hint: Tangent–radius triangle — where is the right angle?
198.help:
Right angle at **point of contact** where **radius ⟂ tangent**.

Example: label 90° at T where tangent meets radius OT.

199.hint: Regular hexagon inscribed — six equal arcs at the centre?
199.help:
Six equal arcs → central angle $360 \div 6 = 60°$ each.

Example: six equal pizza slices from centre.

200.hint: Link all circle words in one paragraph — start from the centre.
200.help:
From **centre**, **radius** to edge; **diameter** is twice radius; **chord** joins two edge points; **tangent** touches once; **arc** is curved edge; **sector** is wedge; **segment** is chord+arc region; **circumference** is full edge.

Example: label each on one diagram.
